metadata.title: Five Questions Before Code: The Calibration Session

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Ciao, Liam — in for Bear. The wrong assumption surfaces before the first file changes? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: The wrong assumption surfaces before the first file changes?
props.segment: Five Questions Before Code: The Calibration

beats.narration_text: She opened Claude Code and asked: what is the one thing about this project that, if I do not tell you, you would probably get wrong on the first build? Claude named it: the project uses an external stylesheet, not inline styles. She had assumed Claude would know — it had read the files. It had read the files and guessed wrong on a key decision. Ten minutes of questions before any code ran would have saved her two hours of debugging. This is Liam, in for Bear.
beats.new_visual_element: ClaudeComposerAsk — cold open, calibration question five of five

props.command: What is the one thing about this project that, if I don't tell you, you'd probably get wrong on the first build?

beats.narration_text: The dangerous moment is when Claude has read the files and looks ready to build. The agentic loop — gather, act, verify — can run for many iterations before reporting back. Wrong actions accumulate before you read the explanation. The temptation to skip calibration is highest exactly when Claude appears most capable. That appearance is the signal to slow down, not to proceed.
beats.new_visual_element: Dangerous moment — Claude reads files / looks ready / agentic loop runs / wrong actions accumulate / slow down
beats.spark_line: Looks ready is the signal to slow down.

beats.narration_text: Five questions, no changes. Question one: what files do you see in this project and what is each for? Question two: what do you think this project is for? Question three: if I asked you to build this specific feature, what would you change? Question four: what would you not touch? Question five: what are you uncertain about that I should clarify before we start? Five questions, ten minutes. The wrong assumption surfaces before the first file changes.
beats.new_visual_element: Five questions — files / purpose / what would change / what not touch / uncertain / no changes / ten minutes
beats.spark_line: Five questions. Ten minutes. No changes.

beats.narration_text: Claude can read files. It cannot read your intent or your project's local exceptions. Intent lives in your head: the audience you are building for, the constraint you accepted at the start, the decision you made last week that is not in any file. Local exceptions are the small deviations from convention that your project depends on. The five questions surface both — because they ask Claude to name its assumptions, and assumptions are where these gaps hide.
beats.new_visual_element: Two things Claude cannot see — intent in your head / local exceptions / assumptions surface both / five questions reveal them
beats.spark_line: Intent and exceptions. Not in the files.

beats.narration_text: The calibration session is not a build session. It is a reading session — you read what Claude sees, correct the wrong assumptions, and start the build with a shared model of the project. The economics: ten minutes of questions, not guessing for two hours. The session you start after the five questions is a session where Claude has the right context and you have the right expectations. That is the only session worth starting.
beats.new_visual_element: Artifact view — five questions / wrong assumption surfaced / corrected before first file change / right context right start
beats.spark_line: Ten minutes of questions. Not two hours of debugging.

beats.narration_text: Run these five questions before your first Claude Code build this week. Paste this.

props.command: Answer these five questions about my project — do not make any changes yet: (1) What files do you see and what is each for? (2) What do you think this project is for? (3) If I asked you to [describe your intended feature], what would you change? (4) What would you not touch? (5) What are you uncertain about that I should clarify before we start?

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Here's what the body just demonstrated. The calibration session is not a build session. It is a reading session — you read what Claude sees, correct the wrong assumptions, and start the build with a shared model of the project. The economics: ten minutes of questions, not guessing for two hours. The session you start after the five questions is a session where Claude has the right context and you have the right expectations. That is the only session worth starting. Run these five questions before your first Claude Code build this week. Paste this.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Five Questions Before Code: The Calibration Session] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
